options.D: Laparotomy, enterotomy proximal to the stone, stone extraction, and repair — with or without fistula repair at the same sitting

explanation.correct: Gallstone ileus (impaction of a large gallstone in the distal ileum through a cholecystoduodenal fistula) is managed by laparotomy with enterotomy proximal to the stone, stone extraction, and bowel repair. Cholecystectomy and fistula closure at the same operation is associated with higher morbidity and is generally deferred to a second operation when the patient has recovered. One-stage surgery is only considered in fit, stable patients at specialist centres.

wrong.A: Conservative management will not resolve a 4cm gallstone obstruction. Large stones do not spontaneously pass through the ileocaecal valve (normal lumen ~2cm). Surgery is mandatory.
wrong.B: Cholecystectomy and fistula repair as the priority would delay stone extraction and risk bowel perforation from the impacted stone. Stone extraction (enterolithotomy) is the immediate surgical priority.
wrong.C: The stone is in the terminal ileum, beyond endoscopic reach. ERCP cannot retrieve it. The cholecystoduodenal fistula may be visible on ERCP but the stone has already passed.

explanation.guideline: WSES Gallstone Ileus Management Guidelines 2019 — enterolithotomy as primary; cholecystectomy deferred to second operation
explanation.trial: Doko M et al., Arch Surg 2003: gallstone ileus — one-stage vs two-stage — one-stage: higher morbidity (47% vs 11%); two-stage preferred: enterolithotomy first, fistula repair electively.
explanation.pearl: Gallstone ileus Rigler's triad: (1) small bowel obstruction, (2) ectopic gallstone, (3) pneumobilia (air in bile ducts). The stone must be ≥2.5cm to cause ileal obstruction. Check for second stone in gallbladder (30%) — if present, fistula repair at second operation removes the source.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child presents with episodic severe colicky abdominal pain, 'currant jelly' stools, and a right upper quadrant mass on palpation. US shows a 'target sign' in the right iliac fossa. What is the condition and first-line treatment?

options.A: Volvulus — emergency laparotomy
options.B: Intussusception — pneumatic or hydrostatic enema reduction
options.C: Intestinal atresia — surgical repair
options.D: Hirschsprung's disease — rectal biopsy and pull-through

explanation.correct: Intussusception (ileocolic) in a toddler presents with the classic triad: colicky pain, currant jelly stools (blood + mucus), and right-sided abdominal mass. US target sign confirms the diagnosis. Non-surgical reduction by pneumatic or hydrostatic enema (under fluoroscopic or US guidance) is the treatment of choice, with 85–90% success rate. Surgery is reserved for failed reduction, peritonitis, or perforation.

wrong.A: Volvulus in children (midgut volvulus from malrotation) presents with bilious vomiting in a neonate and requires emergency surgery. The clinical presentation here is classic for intussusception, not volvulus.
wrong.C: Intestinal atresia presents in the neonatal period with bilious vomiting and failure to pass meconium. Double bubble on AXR is the classic sign. This toddler has episodic pain and blood per rectum.
wrong.D: Hirschsprung's disease presents in neonates with failure to pass meconium and chronic constipation, not acute colicky pain with rectal bleeding. Diagnosis requires suction rectal biopsy.

explanation.guideline: APSA (American Pediatric Surgical Association) Intussusception Guidelines — pneumatic/hydrostatic reduction as first-line; surgery for failure or peritonitis
explanation.trial: Daneman A et al., Pediatr Radiol 1995: pneumatic vs hydrostatic reduction — pneumatic: 90% success, 0.5% perforation; hydrostatic: 85% success, 0.2% perforation; both safe first-line approaches.
explanation.pearl: Intussusception reduction success criteria: complete reduction confirmed by free flow into terminal ileum and decompression of all bowel loops. Contraindications to enema: peritonitis, perforation on AXR (free gas), haemodynamic instability. Recurrence rate after enema: 5-10%; surgery if 3rd recurrence (search for lead point in older children).

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man has an incidental finding of a 1.8cm well-circumscribed submucosal lesion in the mid-jejunum on CT performed for abdominal pain. Capsule endoscopy shows a yellowish submucosal lesion with intact overlying mucosa. He has no carcinoid syndrome symptoms. Chromogranin A is 2× upper limit of normal. What is the most likely diagnosis and next investigation?

options.A: Small bowel carcinoid (neuroendocrine tumour) — somatostatin receptor scintigraphy (Ga-68 DOTATATE PET-CT)
options.B: Lipoma — no further investigation required
options.C: Lymphoma — staging CT and PET-CT
options.D: GIST — CT-guided biopsy

explanation.correct: A submucosal yellowish lesion in the small bowel with elevated chromogranin A is highly suspicious for a small bowel neuroendocrine tumour (NET/carcinoid). Ga-68 DOTATATE PET-CT has 95% sensitivity for NET and is used for staging (mesenteric nodes, liver metastases, lymph nodes) before surgical planning. Small bowel NETs are the commonest small bowel malignancy and frequently present with mesenteric deposits despite small primary tumour size.

wrong.B: Lipoma is echogenic/hypodense on CT (fat density), not yellowish on endoscopy, and does not cause chromogranin A elevation. This presentation is not a lipoma.
wrong.C: Small bowel lymphoma is uncommon and presents with ulcerating mucosal lesions, not a small submucosal yellowish lesion with chromogranin elevation.
wrong.D: GIST is a possibility (submucosal, well-circumscribed) but the yellowish colour and elevated chromogranin A strongly suggest NET rather than GIST. Biopsy of small bowel lesions carries perforation risk — DOTATATE PET-CT is a better next step to characterise and stage.

explanation.guideline: ENETS NET Guidelines 2023 — Ga-68 DOTATATE PET-CT for staging small bowel NET; surgical resection recommended for localised disease
explanation.trial: Hope TA et al., J Nucl Med 2020: Ga-68 DOTATATE PET vs OctreoScan — 90% vs 55% sensitivity for small bowel NET staging; significantly changes management in 35% of cases.
explanation.pearl: Small bowel NETs: even small primaries (<2cm) frequently have regional lymph node and mesenteric metastases (40–60%). Mesenteric root involvement causes desmoplastic reaction, kinking, ischaemia. Carcinoid syndrome (flushing, diarrhoea) only occurs with hepatic metastases. Treatment: surgical resection even in metastatic disease for symptom control and debulking.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man develops sudden severe abdominal pain, vomiting, and distension 3 hours after receiving haemodialysis. He has AF on warfarin (INR 3.8). CT angiography shows an acute SMA embolism at the origin of the middle colic artery, with no pneumatosis and good bowel wall enhancement. His lactate is 1.8 mmol/L. What is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Conservative management — imaging findings are incidental
options.B: Emergency laparotomy and SMA embolectomy
options.C: Catheter-directed thrombolysis or aspiration thrombectomy via SMA
options.D: Systemic anticoagulation with IV heparin only

explanation.correct: Acute SMA embolism without peritonitis, normal lactate, and good bowel wall enhancement on CT is at a stage where revascularisation without bowel resection is possible. Catheter-directed thrombolysis or mechanical aspiration thrombectomy via interventional radiology is the preferred approach in a centre with mesenteric IR expertise. This avoids major surgery in a high-risk patient (AF, warfarin, haemodialysis). Surgery is required if IR fails or clinical deterioration occurs.

wrong.A: An SMA embolism causing acute abdominal symptoms is not an incidental finding. Without treatment, bowel ischaemia will develop within hours. Immediate management is mandatory.
wrong.B: Emergency open SMA embolectomy is appropriate when IR is unavailable, the patient has peritonitis, or bowel viability is in question. This patient has no peritonitis and good bowel enhancement — IR-first is appropriate.
wrong.D: Systemic heparin anticoagulation alone does not lyse the embolus. It prevents propagation but does not restore perfusion. Active revascularisation (IR or surgery) is required.

explanation.guideline: ESVS Acute Mesenteric Ischaemia Guidelines 2020 — catheter-directed thrombolysis for acute SMA embolism without peritonitis
explanation.trial: Acosta S et al., Br J Surg 2014: endovascular treatment for acute AMI — 30-day mortality 36% (vs 56% open); bowel resection avoided in 60% of IR-treated patients.
explanation.pearl: Second-look laparotomy: after revascularisation (surgical or endovascular), a second-look laparotomy at 24–48h is recommended to assess bowel viability. Segments with delayed recovery are resected at second-look. This principle avoids excessive resection at the initial operation when bowel viability is uncertain.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man has a CT scan during workup for weight loss showing a 6cm small bowel mass with an exophytic growth pattern, low attenuation, and cystic change. EUS-FNA is inconclusive. The mass is resected and pathology confirms a GIST (c-kit positive) with a mitotic rate of 8/50 HPF. Margins are clear. What is the recommended adjuvant treatment?

options.A: Adjuvant chemotherapy with FOLFOX
options.B: Adjuvant imatinib for 3 years
options.C: Surveillance CT every 6 months — no adjuvant therapy needed
options.D: Adjuvant radiotherapy to the tumour bed

explanation.correct: High-risk GIST (tumour size ≥5cm + mitotic rate ≥5/50 HPF, or size ≥10cm, or ≥10/50 HPF any size) requires adjuvant imatinib (tyrosine kinase inhibitor, targets c-kit/PDGFRA). ACOSOG Z9001 and SSGXVIII trials show 3 years of adjuvant imatinib reduces recurrence-free survival from 17.9 months (1 year) to improved survival at 3 years. This 6cm GIST with 8/50 HPF mitoses is high-risk.

wrong.A: FOLFOX is colorectal cancer chemotherapy with no role in GIST. GIST is resistant to conventional cytotoxic chemotherapy.
wrong.C: Surveillance only (no adjuvant imatinib) would be appropriate for very low-risk GIST (<2cm, <5 mitoses/50 HPF). This is high-risk GIST — adjuvant imatinib is evidence-based standard.
wrong.D: Radiotherapy has no established role in GIST management. GIST is not radiosensitive, and the small bowel tolerates radiotherapy poorly.

explanation.guideline: ESMO Soft Tissue Sarcoma/GIST Guidelines 2021 — adjuvant imatinib 3 years for high-risk GIST (c-kit positive)
explanation.trial: SSGXVIII trial (Joensuu H, JAMA 2012): 3 vs 1 year adjuvant imatinib for high-risk GIST — 3 years: 5-yr RFS 65.6% vs 47.9%; 5-yr OS 92% vs 81.7%; 3 years is now standard.
explanation.pearl: GIST risk stratification (Miettinen/Lasota): size + mitotic rate + tumour location. Small bowel GISTs have higher malignant potential than gastric GISTs at equivalent size/mitoses. Test for PDGFRA D842V mutation — imatinib-resistant, requires avapritinib. KIT exon 11 deletion — best imatinib response.
